title: Comedy film

infobox.value: is a [film genre](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre") that emphasizes [humor](/wiki/Humour "Humour"). These films are designed to amuse audiences and make them laugh.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Films in this genre typically have a [happy ending](/wiki/Happy_ending "Happy ending"), with [dark comedy](/wiki/Black_comedy "Black comedy") being an exception to this rule. Comedy is one of the oldest genres in film, and it is derived from classical [comedy](/wiki/Comedy "Comedy") in [theatre](/wiki/Theatre "Theatre"). Some of the earliest [silent films](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film") were [slapstick comedies](/wiki/Slapstick_comedy "Slapstick comedy"), which often relied on visual depictions, such as sight gags and pratfalls, so they could be enjoyed without requiring sound. To provide drama and excitement to silent movies, live music was played in sync with the action on the screen, on pianos, organs, and other instruments.[[2]](#cite_note-2) When sound films became more prevalent during the 1920s, comedy films grew in popularity, as laughter could result from both [burlesque](/wiki/Burlesque "Burlesque") situations but also from humorous [dialogue](/wiki/Dialogue "Dialogue").

a random or [stream-of-consciousness](/wiki/Stream_of_consciousness "Stream of consciousness") type of humor that often lampoons a form of authority.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The genre dates from the silent era. Notable examples are films produced by [Monty Python](/wiki/Monty_Python "Monty Python").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Other examples include *[A Night at the Opera](/wiki/A_Night_at_the_Opera_(film) "A Night at the Opera (film)")* (1935) and *[Dirty Work](/wiki/Dirty_Work_(1998_film) "Dirty Work (1998 film)")* (1998).

(or gross-out comedy)**:** [Gross out films](/wiki/Gross_out#Cinema "Gross out") are often aimed at the young adult market (18–24) and rely heavily on vulgar, sexual, or ["toilet" humor](/wiki/Toilet_humour "Toilet humour"). They often contain a large amount of profanity and nudity.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Examples include *[Porky's](/wiki/Porky%27s "Porky's")* (1981) and *[There's Something About Mary](/wiki/There%27s_Something_About_Mary "There's Something About Mary")* (1998).

film deals with [taboo](/wiki/Taboo "Taboo") subjects—including death, murder, crime, suicide, and war—in a satirical manner.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Examples include *[Do the Right Thing](/wiki/Do_the_Right_Thing "Do the Right Thing")* (1989) and *[In Bruges](/wiki/In_Bruges "In Bruges")* (2008).

This sub-type uses comedy to explore serious ideas such as religion, sex, or politics. Often, the characters represent particular divergent worldviews and are forced to interact for comedic effect and social commentary.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Some examples include both *[Wag the Dog](/wiki/Wag_the_Dog "Wag the Dog")* (1997) and *[The Invention of Lying](/wiki/The_Invention_of_Lying "The Invention of Lying")* (2009).

satirizes the mores and affectations of a social class. The plot is often concerned with an illicit love affair or other scandal. Generally, the plot is less important for its comedic effect than its witty dialogue. This form of comedy has a long ancestry that dates back at least as far as *[Much Ado About Nothing](/wiki/Much_Ado_About_Nothing "Much Ado About Nothing")* by [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare"), published in 1623.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Examples include *[Breakfast at Tiffany's](/wiki/Breakfast_at_Tiffany%27s_(film) "Breakfast at Tiffany's (film)")* (1961) and *[Under the Tuscan Sun](/wiki/Under_the_Tuscan_Sun_(film) "Under the Tuscan Sun (film)")* (2003).

[Farcical](/wiki/Farce "Farce") films exaggerate situations beyond the realm of possibility—thereby making them entertaining.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Examples include *[What's Up, Doc?](/wiki/What%27s_Up,_Doc%3F_(1972_film) "What's Up, Doc? (1972 film)")* (1972).

comedies are fictional but use a doc-style that includes interviews and "documentary" footage, along with regular scenes. Examples include *[This Is Spinal Tap](/wiki/This_Is_Spinal_Tap "This Is Spinal Tap")* (1984) and *[I'm Still Here](/wiki/I%27m_Still_Here_(2010_film) "I'm Still Here (2010 film)")* (2010).

a film genre has its roots in the 1920s, with Disney's *[Steamboat Willie](/wiki/Steamboat_Willie "Steamboat Willie")* (1928) being the most popular of these early films. The subgenre resurged with popularity in the 1970s, with movies such as *[Bugsy Malone](/wiki/Bugsy_Malone "Bugsy Malone")* (1976) and *[Grease](/wiki/Grease_(film) "Grease (film)")* (1978) gaining status as [cult classics](/wiki/Cult_film "Cult film").

films find humor in the common practices of everyday life.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Some film examples of observational humor include *[Purely Belter](/wiki/Purely_Belter "Purely Belter")* (2000) and *[The Big Year](/wiki/The_Big_Year "The Big Year")* (2011).

A [parody or spoof film](/wiki/Parody_film "Parody film") satirizes other film genres or classic films. Such films employ sarcasm, stereotyping, mockery of scenes from other films, and the obviousness of meaning in a character's actions.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Examples of this form include *[Young Frankenstein](/wiki/Young_Frankenstein "Young Frankenstein")* (1974) and *[Airplane!](/wiki/Airplane! "Airplane!")* (1980).

The humor is primarily derived from sexual situations and desire,[[17]](#cite_note-17) as in *[Animal House](/wiki/Animal_House "Animal House")* (1978) and *[How to Be a Latin Lover](/wiki/How_to_Be_a_Latin_Lover "How to Be a Latin Lover")* (2017).

where humor comes from knowing a stock group of characters (or character types) and then exposing them to different situations to create humorous and ironic juxtaposition.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Examples include *[After Hours](/wiki/After_Hours_(film) "After Hours (film)")* (1985) and *[Hot Tub Time Machine](/wiki/Hot_Tub_Time_Machine "Hot Tub Time Machine")* (2010).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Genre of film that emphasizes humour

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Modern_Times_poster.jpg/250px-Modern_Times_poster.jpg)](/wiki/File:Modern_Times_poster.jpg)

Poster for [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin")'s film *[Modern Times](/wiki/Modern_Times_(film) "Modern Times (film)")* (1936)

The **comedy film** is a [film genre](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre") that emphasizes [humor](/wiki/Humour "Humour"). These films are designed to amuse audiences and make them laugh.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Films in this genre typically have a [happy ending](/wiki/Happy_ending "Happy ending"), with [dark comedy](/wiki/Black_comedy "Black comedy") being an exception to this rule. Comedy is one of the oldest genres in film, and it is derived from classical [comedy](/wiki/Comedy "Comedy") in [theatre](/wiki/Theatre "Theatre"). Some of the earliest [silent films](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film") were [slapstick comedies](/wiki/Slapstick_comedy "Slapstick comedy"), which often relied on visual depictions, such as sight gags and pratfalls, so they could be enjoyed without requiring sound. To provide drama and excitement to silent movies, live music was played in sync with the action on the screen, on pianos, organs, and other instruments.[[2]](#cite_note-2) When sound films became more prevalent during the 1920s, comedy films grew in popularity, as laughter could result from both [burlesque](/wiki/Burlesque "Burlesque") situations but also from humorous [dialogue](/wiki/Dialogue "Dialogue").

When compared to other [film genres](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre"), comedy films place more emphasis on the individual star actor or actors, with many former [stand-up comics](/wiki/Stand-up_comics "Stand-up comics") transitioning to the film industry due to their popularity.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In *[The Screenwriters Taxonomy](/wiki/Screenwriters_Taxonomy "Screenwriters Taxonomy")* (2017), [Eric R. Williams](/wiki/Eric_R._Williams "Eric R. Williams") contends that film genres are fundamentally based upon a film's atmosphere, character, and story, and therefore, the labels "drama" and "comedy" are too broad to be considered a genre.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) Instead, his taxonomy argues that comedy is a type of film that contains at least a dozen different sub-types.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) A number of hybrid genres have emerged, such as [action comedy](/wiki/Action_comedy "Action comedy") and [romantic comedy](/wiki/Romantic_comedy "Romantic comedy").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comedy_film&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Silent film era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comedy_film&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Silent film era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Cin%C3%A9matographe_Lumi%C3%A8re.jpg/250px-Cin%C3%A9matographe_Lumi%C3%A8re.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cin%C3%A9matographe_Lumi%C3%A8re.jpg)

The film poster for the first comedy film, *[L'Arroseur Arrosé](/wiki/L%27Arroseur_Arros%C3%A9 "L'Arroseur Arrosé")* (1895)

The first comedy film was *[L'Arroseur Arrosé](/wiki/L%27Arroseur_Arros%C3%A9 "L'Arroseur Arrosé")* (1895), directed and produced by film pioneer [Louis Lumière](/wiki/Louis_Lumi%C3%A8re "Louis Lumière"). Less than a minute long, it shows a boy playing a prank on a gardener. The most notable comedy actors of the [silent film era](/wiki/Silent_film_era "Silent film era") (1895–1927) were [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin"), [Harold Lloyd](/wiki/Harold_Lloyd "Harold Lloyd"), and [Buster Keaton](/wiki/Buster_Keaton "Buster Keaton"), though they were able to make the transition into "[talkies](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film")" after the 1920s.

#### Social commentary in comedy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comedy_film&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Social commentary in comedy")]

Film-makers in the 1960s skillfully employed the use of comedy film to make social statements by building their narratives around sensitive cultural, political or social issues. Such films include [Dr Strangelove, or How I Learned to Love the Bomb](/wiki/Dr._Strangelove "Dr. Strangelove"), [Guess Who's Coming to Dinner?](/wiki/Guess_Who%27s_Coming_to_Dinner "Guess Who's Coming to Dinner") and [The Graduate](/wiki/The_Graduate "The Graduate").[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)

#### Camp and bawdy comedy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comedy_film&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Camp and bawdy comedy")]

In America, the [sexual revolution](/wiki/Sexual_revolution "Sexual revolution") drove an appetite for comedies that celebrated and parodied changing social morals, including [Bob & Carol & Ted & Alice](/wiki/Bob_%26_Carol_%26_Ted_%26_Alice "Bob & Carol & Ted & Alice") and [Fanny Hill](/wiki/Fanny_Hill_(1964_film) "Fanny Hill (1964 film)").[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) In Britain, a [camp](/wiki/Camp_(style) "Camp (style)") sensibility lay behind the successful [Carry On films](/wiki/Carry_On_(franchise) "Carry On (franchise)"), while in America subversive independent film-maker [John Waters](/wiki/John_Waters "John Waters") made camp films for college audiences with his [drag queen](/wiki/Drag_queen "Drag queen") friends that eventually found a mainstream audience.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The success of the American television show [Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live") drove decades of cinema with racier content allowed on television drawing on the program's stars and characters, with bigger successes including *[Wayne's World](/wiki/Wayne%27s_World_(film) "Wayne's World (film)")*, *[Mean Girls](/wiki/Mean_Girls "Mean Girls")*, *[Ghostbusters](/wiki/Ghostbusters "Ghostbusters")* and *[Animal House](/wiki/Animal_House "Animal House")*.[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)

### Present era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comedy_film&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Present era")]

Parody and joke-based films continue to find audiences.[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comedy_film&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reception")]

While comedic films are among the most popular with audiences at the box office, there is an 'historical bias against a close and serious consideration of comedy' when it comes to critical reception and conferring of awards, such as at the [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"). Film writer Cailian Savage observes "Comedies have won Oscars, although they’ve usually been comedy-dramas, involved very depressing scenes, or appealed to stone-hearted drama lovers in some other way, such as *[Shakespeare in Love](/wiki/Shakespeare_in_Love "Shakespeare in Love")*."

## Sub-types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comedy_film&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Sub-types")]

* **Anarchic comedy:** a random or [stream-of-consciousness](/wiki/Stream_of_consciousness "Stream of consciousness") type of humor that often lampoons a form of authority.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The genre dates from the silent era. Notable examples are films produced by [Monty Python](/wiki/Monty_Python "Monty Python").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Other examples include *[A Night at the Opera](/wiki/A_Night_at_the_Opera_(film) "A Night at the Opera (film)")* (1935) and *[Dirty Work](/wiki/Dirty_Work_(1998_film) "Dirty Work (1998 film)")* (1998).
* **Bathroom comedy** (or gross-out comedy)**:** [Gross out films](/wiki/Gross_out#Cinema "Gross out") are often aimed at the young adult market (18–24) and rely heavily on vulgar, sexual, or ["toilet" humor](/wiki/Toilet_humour "Toilet humour"). They often contain a large amount of profanity and nudity.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Examples include *[Porky's](/wiki/Porky%27s "Porky's")* (1981) and *[There's Something About Mary](/wiki/There%27s_Something_About_Mary "There's Something About Mary")* (1998).
* **[Black comedy](/wiki/Black_comedy "Black comedy")**: film deals with [taboo](/wiki/Taboo "Taboo") subjects—including death, murder, crime, suicide, and war—in a satirical manner.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Examples include *[Do the Right Thing](/wiki/Do_the_Right_Thing "Do the Right Thing")* (1989) and *[In Bruges](/wiki/In_Bruges "In Bruges")* (2008).
* **Comedy of ideas:** This sub-type uses comedy to explore serious ideas such as religion, sex, or politics. Often, the characters represent particular divergent worldviews and are forced to interact for comedic effect and social commentary.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Some examples include both *[Wag the Dog](/wiki/Wag_the_Dog "Wag the Dog")* (1997) and *[The Invention of Lying](/wiki/The_Invention_of_Lying "The Invention of Lying")* (2009).
* **[Comedy of manners](/wiki/Comedy_of_manners "Comedy of manners")**: satirizes the mores and affectations of a social class. The plot is often concerned with an illicit love affair or other scandal. Generally, the plot is less important for its comedic effect than its witty dialogue. This form of comedy has a long ancestry that dates back at least as far as *[Much Ado About Nothing](/wiki/Much_Ado_About_Nothing "Much Ado About Nothing")* by [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare"), published in 1623.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Examples include *[Breakfast at Tiffany's](/wiki/Breakfast_at_Tiffany%27s_(film) "Breakfast at Tiffany's (film)")* (1961) and *[Under the Tuscan Sun](/wiki/Under_the_Tuscan_Sun_(film) "Under the Tuscan Sun (film)")* (2003).
* **Farce:** [Farcical](/wiki/Farce "Farce") films exaggerate situations beyond the realm of possibility—thereby making them entertaining.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Examples include *[What's Up, Doc?](/wiki/What%27s_Up,_Doc%3F_(1972_film) "What's Up, Doc? (1972 film)")* (1972).
* **[Mockumentary](/wiki/Mockumentary "Mockumentary"):** comedies are fictional but use a doc-style that includes interviews and "documentary" footage, along with regular scenes. Examples include *[This Is Spinal Tap](/wiki/This_Is_Spinal_Tap "This Is Spinal Tap")* (1984) and *[I'm Still Here](/wiki/I%27m_Still_Here_(2010_film) "I'm Still Here (2010 film)")* (2010).
* **Musical comedy**: a film genre has its roots in the 1920s, with Disney's *[Steamboat Willie](/wiki/Steamboat_Willie "Steamboat Willie")* (1928) being the most popular of these early films. The subgenre resurged with popularity in the 1970s, with movies such as *[Bugsy Malone](/wiki/Bugsy_Malone "Bugsy Malone")* (1976) and *[Grease](/wiki/Grease_(film) "Grease (film)")* (1978) gaining status as [cult classics](/wiki/Cult_film "Cult film").
* **[Observational comedy](/wiki/Observational_comedy "Observational comedy"):** films find humor in the common practices of everyday life.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Some film examples of observational humor include *[Purely Belter](/wiki/Purely_Belter "Purely Belter")* (2000) and *[The Big Year](/wiki/The_Big_Year "The Big Year")* (2011).
* **Parody (or spoof):** A [parody or spoof film](/wiki/Parody_film "Parody film") satirizes other film genres or classic films. Such films employ sarcasm, stereotyping, mockery of scenes from other films, and the obviousness of meaning in a character's actions.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Examples of this form include *[Young Frankenstein](/wiki/Young_Frankenstein "Young Frankenstein")* (1974) and *[Airplane!](/wiki/Airplane! "Airplane!")* (1980).
* **[Sex comedy](/wiki/Sex_comedy "Sex comedy"):** The humor is primarily derived from sexual situations and desire,[[17]](#cite_note-17) as in *[Animal House](/wiki/Animal_House "Animal House")* (1978) and *[How to Be a Latin Lover](/wiki/How_to_Be_a_Latin_Lover "How to Be a Latin Lover")* (2017).
* **[Sitcom](/wiki/Sitcom "Sitcom"):** where humor comes from knowing a stock group of characters (or character types) and then exposing them to different situations to create humorous and ironic juxtaposition.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Examples include *[After Hours](/wiki/After_Hours_(film) "After Hours (film)")* (1985) and *[Hot Tub Time Machine](/wiki/Hot_Tub_Time_Machine "Hot Tub Time Machine")* (2010).
* **Straight comedy:** This broad sub-type applies to films that do not attempt a specific approach to comedy but, rather, use comedy for comedic sake.[[19]](#cite_note-:3-19) *[Anger Management](/wiki/Anger_Management_(film) "Anger Management (film)")* (2003) and *[Bridesmaids](/wiki/Bridesmaids_(2011_film) "Bridesmaids (2011 film)")* (2011) are examples of straight comedy films.
* **[Slapstick film](/wiki/Slapstick_film "Slapstick film"):** involve exaggerated, boisterous physical action to create impossible and humorous situations. Because it relies predominantly on visual depictions of events, it does not require sound. Accordingly, the subgenre was ideal for silent movies and was prevalent during that era.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Stars of slapstick include [Harold Lloyd](/wiki/Harold_Lloyd "Harold Lloyd"), [Roscoe Arbuckle](/wiki/Roscoe_Arbuckle "Roscoe Arbuckle"), [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin"), [Peter Sellers](/wiki/Peter_Sellers "Peter Sellers") and [Norman Wisdom](/wiki/Norman_Wisdom "Norman Wisdom"). Some of these stars, as well as acts such as [Laurel and Hardy](/wiki/Laurel_and_Hardy "Laurel and Hardy") and [the Three Stooges](/wiki/The_Three_Stooges "The Three Stooges"), also found success incorporating [slapstick comedy](/wiki/Slapstick_comedy "Slapstick comedy") into sound films. Modern examples of slapstick comedy include *[Mouse Hunt](/wiki/Mouse_Hunt "Mouse Hunt")* (1997) and *[Nacho Libre](/wiki/Nacho_Libre "Nacho Libre")* (2006).
* **[Surreal humor](/wiki/Surreal_humor "Surreal humor"):** Although not specifically linked to the history of [surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"), [surreal comedies](/wiki/Surreal_humor "Surreal humor") include behavior and storytelling techniques that are illogical—including bizarre [juxtapositions](/wiki/Juxtaposition "Juxtaposition"), absurd situations, and unpredictable reactions to normal situations.[[19]](#cite_note-:3-19) Some examples are *[Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_(1985_film) "Brazil (1985 film)")* (1985) and *[Barton Fink](/wiki/Barton_Fink "Barton Fink")* (1991).

## Hybrid sub-genres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comedy_film&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hybrid sub-genres")]

According to [Williams' taxonomy](/wiki/Williams%27_taxonomy "Williams' taxonomy"), all film descriptions should contain their type (comedy or drama) combined with one (or more) sub-genres.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) This combination does not create a separate genre, but rather, provides a better understanding of the film.

* **[Action comedy](/wiki/Action_comedy "Action comedy"):** Films of this type blend comic antics and action where the stars combine one-liners with a thrilling plot and daring stunts. The genre became a specific draw in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") in the 1980s when comedians such as [Eddie Murphy](/wiki/Eddie_Murphy "Eddie Murphy") started taking more action-oriented roles, such as in *[48 Hrs.](/wiki/48_Hrs. "48 Hrs.")* (1982) and *[Beverly Hills Cop](/wiki/Beverly_Hills_Cop "Beverly Hills Cop")* (1984).
* Sub-genres of the action comedy (labeled macro-genres by Williams) include:[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)
  + **[Martial arts films](/wiki/Martial_arts_films "Martial arts films"):** [Slapstick](/wiki/Slapstick "Slapstick") [martial arts films](/wiki/Martial_arts_films "Martial arts films") became a mainstay of [Hong Kong action cinema](/wiki/Hong_Kong_action_cinema "Hong Kong action cinema") through the work of [Jackie Chan](/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan") among others, such as *[Who Am I?](/wiki/Who_Am_I%3F_(1998_film) "Who Am I? (1998 film)")* (1998). *[Kung Fu Panda](/wiki/Kung_Fu_Panda_(franchise) "Kung Fu Panda (franchise)")* is an action comedy that focuses on the martial art of [kung fu](/wiki/Chinese_martial_arts "Chinese martial arts").
  + **[Superhero films](/wiki/Superhero_films "Superhero films"):** Some action films focus on [superheroes](/wiki/Superhero "Superhero"); for example, *[The Incredibles](/wiki/The_Incredibles "The Incredibles")*, *[Hancock](/wiki/Hancock_(film) "Hancock (film)")*, *[Kick-Ass](/wiki/Kick-Ass_(film) "Kick-Ass (film)")*, and *[Deadpool](/wiki/Deadpool_(film) "Deadpool (film)")*.
  + [**Buddy films:**](/wiki/Buddy_film "Buddy film") Films starring mismatched partners for comedic effects, such as in *[Midnight Run](/wiki/Midnight_Run "Midnight Run")*, *[Rush Hour](/wiki/Rush_Hour_(1998_film) "Rush Hour (1998 film)")*, *[21 Jump Street](/wiki/21_Jump_Street_(film) "21 Jump Street (film)")*, *[Bad Boys](/wiki/Bad_Boys_(1995_film) "Bad Boys (1995 film)")*, *[Starsky and Hutch](/wiki/Starsky_and_Hutch_(film) "Starsky and Hutch (film)")*, *[Tango & Cash](/wiki/Tango_%26_Cash "Tango & Cash")*, *[Lethal Weapon](/wiki/Lethal_Weapon "Lethal Weapon")*, and *[Stakeout](/wiki/Stakeout_(1987_film) "Stakeout (1987 film)")*.
* **[Comedy thriller](/wiki/Comedy_thriller "Comedy thriller"):** Comedy thriller is a type that combines elements of humor and suspense. Films such as *[Silver Streak](/wiki/Silver_Streak_(film) "Silver Streak (film)")*, *[Charade](/wiki/Charade_(1963_film) "Charade (1963 film)")*, *[Kiss Kiss Bang Bang](/wiki/Kiss_Kiss_Bang_Bang "Kiss Kiss Bang Bang")*, *[American Psycho](/wiki/American_Psycho_(film) "American Psycho (film)")*, *[Mr. and Mrs. Smith](/wiki/Mr._%26_Mrs._Smith_(2005_film) "Mr. & Mrs. Smith (2005 film)")*, *[Grosse Pointe Blank](/wiki/Grosse_Pointe_Blank "Grosse Pointe Blank")*, *[The Thin Man](/wiki/The_Thin_Man_(film) "The Thin Man (film)")*, *[The Big Fix](/wiki/The_Big_Fix_(1978_film) "The Big Fix (1978 film)")*, and *[The Lady Vanishes](/wiki/The_Lady_Vanishes_(1938_film) "The Lady Vanishes (1938 film)")*.
* **Comedy mystery:** Comedy mystery is a film genre combining elements of comedy and mystery fiction. Though the genre arguably peaked in the 1930s and 1940s, comedy-mystery films have been continually produced since.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Examples include the *[Pink Panther](/wiki/Pink_Panther "Pink Panther")* series,[[21]](#cite_note-21) *[Scooby-Doo](/wiki/Scooby-Doo_in_film "Scooby-Doo in film")* films, *[Clue](/wiki/Clue_(film) "Clue (film)")* (1985) and *[Knives Out](/wiki/Knives_Out "Knives Out")* (2019). See also [List of comedy-mystery films](/wiki/List_of_comedy-mystery_films "List of comedy-mystery films")
* **[Crime comedy](/wiki/Crime_comedy "Crime comedy"):** A hybrid mix of crime and comedy films, examples include *[Inspector Palmu's Mistake](/wiki/Inspector_Palmu%27s_Mistake_(film) "Inspector Palmu's Mistake (film)")* (1960), *[Oh Brother Where Art Thou?](/wiki/O_Brother,_Where_Art_Thou%3F "O Brother, Where Art Thou?")* (2000), *[Take the Money and Run](/wiki/Take_the_Money_and_Run_(film) "Take the Money and Run (film)")* (1969) and *[Who Framed Roger Rabbit](/wiki/Who_Framed_Roger_Rabbit "Who Framed Roger Rabbit")* (1988).
* **[Fantasy comedy](/wiki/Fantasy_comedy "Fantasy comedy"):** Fantasy comedy films use [magic](/wiki/Magic_(fantasy) "Magic (fantasy)"), [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural") or mythological figures for comedic purposes. Some fantasy comedy includes an element of parody, or satire, turning fantasy conventions on their head, such as the hero becoming a cowardly fool or the princess being a klutz. Examples of these films include *[Big](/wiki/Big_(film) "Big (film)")*, *[Being John Malkovich](/wiki/Being_John_Malkovich "Being John Malkovich")*, *[Ted](/wiki/Ted_(film) "Ted (film)")*, *[Hook](/wiki/Hook_(film) "Hook (film)")*, *[Night at the Museum](/wiki/Night_at_the_Museum "Night at the Museum")*, *[Groundhog Day](/wiki/Groundhog_Day_(film) "Groundhog Day (film)")*, *[Click](/wiki/Click_(2006_film) "Click (2006 film)")*, and *[A Thousand Words](/wiki/A_Thousand_Words_(film) "A Thousand Words (film)")*.
* **[Comedy horror](/wiki/Comedy_horror "Comedy horror"):** Comedy horror is a genre/type in which the usual dark themes and "scare tactics" attributed to horror films are treated with a humorous approach. These films either often goofy horror cliches, such as in *[Scream](/wiki/Scream_(franchise) "Scream (franchise)")*, *[The Rocky Horror Picture Show](/wiki/The_Rocky_Horror_Picture_Show "The Rocky Horror Picture Show")*, *[Little Shop of Horrors](/wiki/Little_Shop_of_Horrors_(1986_film) "Little Shop of Horrors (1986 film)")*, *[Dracula: Dead and Loving It](/wiki/Dracula:_Dead_and_Loving_It "Dracula: Dead and Loving It")*, and *[Scary Movie](/wiki/Scary_Movie "Scary Movie")* where campy styles are favored. Some are much more subtle and do not parody horror, such as *[An American Werewolf in London](/wiki/An_American_Werewolf_in_London "An American Werewolf in London")*. Another style of comedy horror can also rely on over-the-top violence and gore such as in *[The Evil Dead](/wiki/The_Evil_Dead "The Evil Dead")* (1981), *[The Return of the Living Dead](/wiki/The_Return_of_the_Living_Dead "The Return of the Living Dead")* (1985), *[Braindead](/wiki/Braindead_(film) "Braindead (film)")* (1992), and *[Doghouse](/wiki/Doghouse_(film) "Doghouse (film)")* (2009) – such films are sometimes known as *splatstick*, a portmanteau of the words *splatter* and *slapstick*. It would be reasonable to put *[Ghostbusters](/wiki/Ghostbusters "Ghostbusters")* in this category.
* **[Day-in-the-life](/wiki/Day_in_the_life "Day in the life") comedy:** Day-in-the-life films take small events in a person's life and raise their level of importance. The "small things in life" feel as important to the protagonist (and the audience) as the climactic battle in an action film, or the final shootout in a western.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) Often, the [protagonists](/wiki/Protagonist "Protagonist") deal with multiple, overlapping issues in the course of the film.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)  The day-in-the-life comedy often finds humor in commenting upon the absurdity or irony of daily life; for example *[The Terminal](/wiki/The_Terminal "The Terminal")* (2004) or *[Waitress](/wiki/Waitress_(2007_film) "Waitress (2007 film)")* (2007). Character humor is also used extensively in day-in-the-life comedies, as can be seen in *[American Splendor](/wiki/American_Splendor_(film) "American Splendor (film)")* (2003).
* **[Romantic comedy](/wiki/Romantic_comedy "Romantic comedy"):** Romantic comedies are humorous films with central themes that reinforce societal beliefs about love (e.g., themes such as "[love at first sight](/wiki/Love_at_first_sight "Love at first sight")", "love conquers all", or "there is someone out there for everyone"); the story typically revolves around characters falling into (and out of, and back into) love.[[22]](#cite_note-:4-22) *[Amélie](/wiki/Am%C3%A9lie "Amélie")* (2001), *[Annie Hall](/wiki/Annie_Hall "Annie Hall")* (1977), *[Charade](/wiki/Charade_(1963_film) "Charade (1963 film)")* (1963), *[City Lights](/wiki/City_Lights "City Lights")* (1931), *[Four Weddings and a Funeral](/wiki/Four_Weddings_and_a_Funeral "Four Weddings and a Funeral")* (1994), *[It](/wiki/It_(1927_film) "It (1927 film)")* (1927), *[The Lobster](/wiki/The_Lobster "The Lobster")* (2015), *[My Wife, the Director General](/wiki/My_Wife,_the_Director_General "My Wife, the Director General")* (1966), *[My Favorite Wife](/wiki/My_Favorite_Wife "My Favorite Wife")* (1940), *[Pretty Woman](/wiki/Pretty_Woman "Pretty Woman")* (1990), *[Some Like It Hot](/wiki/Some_Like_It_Hot "Some Like It Hot")* (1959), *[Something's Gotta Give](/wiki/Something%27s_Gotta_Give_(film) "Something's Gotta Give (film)")* (2003) and *[When Harry Met Sally...](/wiki/When_Harry_Met_Sally... "When Harry Met Sally...")* (1989) are examples of romantic comedies.
* **[Screwball comedy](/wiki/Screwball_comedy "Screwball comedy"):** A subgenre of the romantic comedy, [screwball comedies](/wiki/Screwball_comedy "Screwball comedy") appears to focus on the story of a central male character until a strong female character takes center stage; at this point, the man's story becomes secondary to a new issue typically introduced by the woman; this story grows in significance and, as it does, the man's masculinity is challenged by the sharp-witted woman, who is often his love interest.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) Typically it can include a romantic element, an interplay between people of different economic strata, quick and witty [repartee](/wiki/Repartee "Repartee"), some form of role reversal, and a happy ending. Some examples of screwball comedy during its heyday include *[It Happened One Night](/wiki/It_Happened_One_Night "It Happened One Night")* (1934), *[Bringing Up Baby](/wiki/Bringing_Up_Baby "Bringing Up Baby")* (1938), *[The Philadelphia Story](/wiki/The_Philadelphia_Story_(film) "The Philadelphia Story (film)")* (1940), *[His Girl Friday](/wiki/His_Girl_Friday "His Girl Friday")* (1940), *[Mr. & Mrs. Smith](/wiki/Mr._%26_Mrs._Smith_(1941_film) "Mr. & Mrs. Smith (1941 film)")* (1941); more recent examples include *[Continental Divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide_(film) "Continental Divide (film)")* (1981), [*Rat Race*](/wiki/Rat_Race_(film) "Rat Race (film)") (2001), and *[Our Idiot Brother](/wiki/Our_Idiot_Brother "Our Idiot Brother")* (2011).
* **[Science fiction comedy](/wiki/Science_fiction_comedy "Science fiction comedy"):** Science fiction comedy films often exaggerate the elements of traditional [science fiction films](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film") to comic effect. Examples include *[Spaceballs](/wiki/Spaceballs "Spaceballs")*, *[Coneheads](/wiki/Coneheads_(film) "Coneheads (film)")*, *[Galaxy Quest](/wiki/Galaxy_Quest "Galaxy Quest")*, *[Mars Attacks!](/wiki/Mars_Attacks! "Mars Attacks!")*, *[Men in Black](/wiki/Men_in_Black_(series) "Men in Black (series)")*, and many more.
* **[Sports comedy](/wiki/Sports_comedy "Sports comedy"):** Sports comedy combines the genre of comedy with that of the [sports film](/wiki/Sports_film "Sports film") genre. Thematically, the story is often one of "Our Team" versus "Their Team"; their team will always try to win, and our team will show the world that they deserve recognition or redemption; the story does not always have to involve a team.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) The story could also be about an individual athlete or the story could focus on an individual playing on a team. The comedic aspect of this super-genre often comes from physical humor (*[BASEketball](/wiki/BASEketball "BASEketball")* - 1998), character humor (*[Space Jam](/wiki/Space_Jam "Space Jam")* - 1996), or the juxtaposition of bad athletes succeeding against the odds (*[The Benchwarmers](/wiki/The_Benchwarmers "The Benchwarmers")* - 2006).
* **[War comedy](/wiki/War_comedy "War comedy"):** War films typically tell the story of a small group of isolated individuals who – one by one – get killed (literally or metaphorically) by an outside force until there is a final fight to the death; the idea of the protagonists facing death is a central expectation in a war film.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) War comedies infuse this idea of confronting death with a morbid sense of humor. In a war film even though the enemy may out-number, or out-power, the hero, we assume that the enemy *can* be defeated if only the hero can figure out how.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Often, this strategic sensibility provides humorous opportunities in a war comedy. Examples include *[Good Morning, Vietnam](/wiki/Good_Morning,_Vietnam "Good Morning, Vietnam")*; *[M\*A\*S\*H](/wiki/M*A*S*H "M*A*S*H")*; *[Stripes](/wiki/Stripes_(film) "Stripes (film)")* and others.
* **[Western comedy](/wiki/Western_comedy "Western comedy"):** Films in the Western super-genre often take place in the American Southwest or Mexico, with a large number of scenes occurring outside so we can soak in nature's rugged beauty.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) Visceral expectations for the audience include fistfights, gunplay, and chase scenes. There is also the expectation of spectacular panoramic images of the countryside including sunsets, wide open landscapes, and endless deserts and sky.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) Western comedies often find their humor in specific characters (*[Three Amigos](/wiki/Three_Amigos "Three Amigos")*, 1986), in interpersonal relationships (*[The Lone Ranger](/wiki/The_Lone_Ranger_(2013_film) "The Lone Ranger (2013 film)")*, 2013) or in creating a parody of the western (*[Rango](/wiki/Rango_(2011_film) "Rango (2011 film)")*, 2011).
